topic.what: Review of rising antibiotic resistance in H. pylori, focusing on clarithromycin and metronidazole resistance and their impact on eradication therapy failure rates.
topic.how: Expert synthesis of global surveillance data on H. pylori resistance patterns, mechanisms of resistance acquisition, and clinical consequences for treatment outcomes.
topic.why_matters: Antibiotic resistance is the primary driver of H. pylori eradication failure, threatening the effectiveness of standard triple therapy and necessitating susceptibility-guided or empiric alternative regimens.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Trend in H. pylori resistance rates to clarithromycin and metronidazole over time
characteristics.vs_baseline: Resistance rates substantially higher than in the 1990s when triple therapy was established
characteristics.evidence: Surveillance studies and clinical trial failure rate data across multiple regions
characteristics.context: Resistance rates vary significantly by geography; some regions exceed 20% clarithromycin resistance

good_for: Understanding why standard triple therapy is failing in many regions
Justifying susceptibility testing before empiric therapy
Informing antibiotic stewardship policies for H. pylori treatment
not_for: Regions with documented low resistance rates where triple therapy remains effective
requires: Access to local or regional H. pylori resistance surveillance data
Culture or molecular susceptibility testing infrastructure for personalized therapy

novelty: Comprehensive early synthesis of the global H. pylori resistance crisis and its clinical implications
Clear articulation of why empiric triple therapy should no longer be considered universally first-line
open_problems: Scalable point-of-care molecular resistance testing for H. pylori
Strategies to slow resistance emergence (antibiotic stewardship in H. pylori context)
Development of novel non-antibiotic approaches to H. pylori eradication
